title: File Handling (ifstream, ofstream, fstream)

seo.title: C++ File Handling - ifstream, ofstream, fstream, Binary I/O, File Modes | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master C++ file handling with ifstream, ofstream, fstream. Learn file modes, binary I/O, seekg, seekp, getline, CSV parsing, and student record systems. Includes 52+ practice questions.
seo.keywords: c++ file handling, c++ ifstream, c++ ofstream, c++ fstream, c++ binary file io, c++ read file, c++ write file, c++ getline, c++ file modes, c++ seekg seekp

content.what:
What Is File Handling in C++?

File handling allows C++ programs to read data from files and write data to files on disk. Instead of losing all data when a program terminates, you can persist student records, configuration settings, logs, and any structured data to files.

C++ provides file handling through the <fstream> header, which defines three stream classes:

  • ifstream (input file stream): Read from files
  • ofstream (output file stream): Write to files
  • fstream (file stream): Both read and write
#include <iostream>
#include <fstream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    // Write to a file
    ofstream outFile("students.txt");
    outFile << "Arjun 85" << endl;
    outFile << "Priya 92" << endl;
    outFile << "Kiran 78" << endl;
    outFile.close();

    // Read from the file
    ifstream inFile("students.txt");
    string name;
    int marks;
    while (inFile >> name >> marks) {
        cout << name << " scored " << marks << endl;
    }
    inFile.close();
    return 0;
}

File streams inherit from the same iostream hierarchy as cin and cout. Everything Raghav knows about <<, >>, getline, and stream state applies directly to file streams.
content.why:
Why Is File Handling Important?

1. Data Persistence

Without files, all program data is lost when the program exits. File handling lets Arjun save student records, configuration settings, and computation results that survive beyond the program's lifetime.

2. Practical Applications

Nearly every real-world application involves files: reading configuration files, parsing CSV data, logging errors, saving game state, processing large datasets that do not fit in memory, and communicating between programs through files.

3. Binary File I/O for Performance

Text files are human-readable but slow for large data. Binary files store data in the same format as memory, making reads and writes significantly faster. Database engines, image processors, and serialization libraries all use binary I/O.

4. Foundation for Advanced Topics

Understanding file streams prepares you for network streams, string streams (stringstream), and the C++17 <filesystem> library. The iostream abstraction is the same -- once you understand file I/O, you understand all C++ I/O.

5. Competitive Programming and Interviews

While competitive programming typically uses cin/cout, many online judges and companies use file-based I/O (freopen). Interview questions often involve parsing files, implementing CSV readers, or building config file parsers.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. Opening and Closing Files

Files can be opened through the constructor or the open() method:

#include <iostream>
#include <fstream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    // Method 1: Open via constructor
    ofstream file1("output.txt");
    file1 << "Hello from constructor" << endl;
    file1.close();

    // Method 2: Open via open()
    ofstream file2;
    file2.open("output2.txt");
    if (file2.is_open()) {
        file2 << "Hello from open()" << endl;
        file2.close();
    } else {
        cerr << "Failed to open file" << endl;
    }

    // Destructor auto-closes, but explicit close is good practice
    {
        ofstream file3("output3.txt");
        file3 << "Auto-closed when file3 goes out of scope";
    } // file3.close() called automatically here
    return 0;
}

2. File Modes

File modes control how a file is opened:

ModeDescription
ios::inOpen for reading (default for ifstream)
ios::outOpen for writing (default for ofstream, truncates file)
ios::appAppend to end of file (does not truncate)
ios::binaryBinary mode (no text translation)
ios::truncTruncate file to zero length on open
ios::ateSeek to end after opening
#include <fstream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    // Truncate and write (default for ofstream)
    ofstream f1("data.txt");
    f1 << "First write" << endl;
    f1.close();

    // Append mode: adds to existing content
    ofstream f2("data.txt", ios::app);
    f2 << "Appended line" << endl;
    f2.close();

    // Binary mode for raw data
    ofstream f3("data.bin", ios::binary);
    int num = 42;
    f3.write(reinterpret_cast<char*>(&num), sizeof(num));
    f3.close();

    // Combining modes with |
    fstream f4("data.txt", ios::in | ios::out | ios::app);
    f4.close();
    return 0;
}

3. Reading Line by Line (getline) and Word by Word (>>)

#include <iostream>
#include <fstream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    // Create a test file
    ofstream out("poem.txt");
    out << "Twinkle twinkle little star" << endl;
    out << "How I wonder what you are" << endl;
    out.close();

    // Read line by line
    ifstream in1("poem.txt");
    string line;
    cout << "Line by line:" << endl;
    while (getline(in1, line)) {
        cout << "  " << line << endl;
    }
    in1.close();

    // Read word by word
    ifstream in2("poem.txt");
    string word;
    cout << "Word by word:" << endl;
    while (in2 >> word) {
        cout << "[" << word << "] ";
    }
    cout << endl;
    in2.close();
    return 0;
}

4. Checking File State

#include <iostream>
#include <fstream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    ifstream file("nonexistent.txt");

    // is_open(): was the file successfully opened?
    if (!file.is_open()) {
        cerr << "Could not open file" << endl;
    }

    // Stream state flags
    ifstream f("test.txt");
    // f.good()  -- no errors
    // f.eof()   -- end of file reached
    // f.fail()  -- logical error (e.g., reading int but found string)
    // f.bad()   -- serious I/O error (e.g., disk failure)

    // Common pattern: read until failure
    ifstream data("numbers.txt");
    int num;
    while (data >> num) {
        cout << num << " ";
    }
    if (data.eof()) cout << "\nReached end of file" << endl;
    if (data.fail()) cout << "Read failed (format error or EOF)" << endl;
    return 0;
}

5. Binary File I/O

#include <iostream>
#include <fstream>
#include <cstring>
using namespace std;

struct Student {
    char name[50];
    int age;
    double gpa;
};

int main() {
    // Write binary
    Student s1;
    strcpy(s1.name, "Arjun");
    s1.age = 20;
    s1.gpa = 8.5;

    Student s2;
    strcpy(s2.name, "Priya");
    s2.age = 21;
    s2.gpa = 9.2;

    ofstream out("students.dat", ios::binary);
    out.write(reinterpret_cast<char*>(&s1), sizeof(Student));
    out.write(reinterpret_cast<char*>(&s2), sizeof(Student));
    out.close();

    // Read binary
    ifstream in("students.dat", ios::binary);
    Student temp;
    while (in.read(reinterpret_cast<char*>(&temp), sizeof(Student))) {
        cout << temp.name << ", Age: " << temp.age
             << ", GPA: " << temp.gpa << endl;
    }
    in.close();
    return 0;
}

6. File Position: seekg, seekp, tellg, tellp

#include <iostream>
#include <fstream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    // seekg: set get (read) position
    // seekp: set put (write) position
    // tellg: get current read position
    // tellp: get current write position

    ofstream out("seektest.txt");
    out << "ABCDEFGHIJ";
    out.close();

    ifstream in("seektest.txt");

    // Read from position 3
    in.seekg(3);  // move to 4th byte (0-indexed)
    char ch;
    in.get(ch);
    cout << "At position 3: " << ch << endl;  // D

    // Tell current position
    cout << "Current position: " << in.tellg() << endl;  // 4

    // Seek from end
    in.seekg(-3, ios::end);
    in.get(ch);
    cout << "3 from end: " << ch << endl;  // H

    // Seek from current position
    in.seekg(1, ios::cur);
    in.get(ch);
    cout << "Skip 1 from current: " << ch << endl;  // J

    // Get file size
    in.seekg(0, ios::end);
    cout << "File size: " << in.tellg() << " bytes" << endl;
    in.close();
    return 0;
}

7. Practical Example: CSV Reader/Writer

#include <iostream>
#include <fstream>
#include <sstream>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
using namespace std;

struct Student {
    string name;
    int age;
    double gpa;
};

void writeCSV(const string& filename, const vector<Student>& students) {
    ofstream file(filename);
    file << "Name,Age,GPA" << endl;  // header
    for (const auto& s : students) {
        file << s.name << "," << s.age << "," << s.gpa << endl;
    }
    file.close();
}

vector<Student> readCSV(const string& filename) {
    vector<Student> students;
    ifstream file(filename);
    string line;
    getline(file, line);  // skip header

    while (getline(file, line)) {
        stringstream ss(line);
        Student s;
        string token;
        getline(ss, s.name, ',');
        getline(ss, token, ',');
        s.age = stoi(token);
        getline(ss, token, ',');
        s.gpa = stod(token);
        students.push_back(s);
    }
    file.close();
    return students;
}

int main() {
    vector<Student> students = {
        {"Arjun", 20, 8.5}, {"Priya", 21, 9.2}, {"Kiran", 19, 7.8}
    };
    writeCSV("students.csv", students);

    auto loaded = readCSV("students.csv");
    for (const auto& s : loaded) {
        cout << s.name << ", Age: " << s.age << ", GPA: " << s.gpa << endl;
    }
    return 0;
}

8. Practical Example: Config File Parser

#include <iostream>
#include <fstream>
#include <sstream>
#include <string>
#include <map>
using namespace std;

map<string, string> parseConfig(const string& filename) {
    map<string, string> config;
    ifstream file(filename);
    string line;

    while (getline(file, line)) {
        // Skip empty lines and comments
        if (line.empty() || line[0] == '#') continue;

        size_t pos = line.find('=');
        if (pos != string::npos) {
            string key = line.substr(0, pos);
            string value = line.substr(pos + 1);
            config[key] = value;
        }
    }
    file.close();
    return config;
}

int main() {
    // Create a config file
    ofstream out("app.conf");
    out << "# Application Config" << endl;
    out << "db_host=localhost" << endl;
    out << "db_port=3306" << endl;
    out << "app_name=StudentPortal" << endl;
    out << "debug=true" << endl;
    out.close();

    auto config = parseConfig("app.conf");
    for (auto& [key, value] : config) {
        cout << key << " = " << value << endl;
    }
    return 0;
}

codeExamples.title: Student Record System with Binary File I/O
codeExamples.explanation: This demonstrates a complete student record system using binary file I/O. Students are stored as fixed-size structs, making random access possible. reinterpret_cast<char*> converts the struct pointer to a byte pointer for write and read. The ios::app flag ensures new records are appended without overwriting existing data.
codeExamples.output:
All Students:
Roll: 101, Name: Arjun, Marks: 85.5
Roll: 102, Name: Priya, Marks: 92
Roll: 103, Name: Kiran, Marks: 78.3

Found: Priya, Marks: 92

codeExamples.title: CSV Reader with stringstream Parsing
codeExamples.explanation: CSV parsing combines getline (to read full lines) with stringstream (to split by commas). Each line is fed into a stringstream, and getline(ss, token, ',') extracts comma-separated fields. This pattern is essential for processing structured data files.
codeExamples.output:
Student Report:
---------------------------
Arjun: Total=253, Average=84.3333
Priya: Total=275, Average=91.6667
Kiran: Total=240, Average=80

codeExamples.title: File Copy Program (Binary Mode)
codeExamples.explanation: This demonstrates binary file copying with buffered reads. in.read(buffer, size) reads up to size bytes. in.gcount() returns the actual number of bytes read (which may be less than the buffer size for the last chunk). This pattern is efficient for copying large files.
codeExamples.output:
This is the source file content.
It has multiple lines.

codeExamples.title: Log File with Timestamp and Append Mode
codeExamples.explanation: This Logger class uses ios::app to append each log entry without overwriting previous entries. Each call opens the file, writes, and closes it, ensuring data is flushed even if the program crashes. This is a common pattern for application logging.
codeExamples.output:
Log contents:
[Thu Apr 10 12:00:00 2026] INFO: Application started
[Thu Apr 10 12:00:00 2026] INFO: User Arjun logged in
[Thu Apr 10 12:00:00 2026] WARN: Memory usage above 80%
[Thu Apr 10 12:00:00 2026] ERROR: Database connection failed
[Thu Apr 10 12:00:00 2026] INFO: Retrying connection...

codeExamples.title: Random Access in Binary File (Update a Specific Record)
codeExamples.explanation: Binary files with fixed-size records allow random access. To read or update record N, seek to position N * sizeof(Record). seekg moves the read position and seekp moves the write position. This is how simple database-like systems work -- O(1) access to any record by index.
codeExamples.output:
Before: Priya, Marks: 78
101: Arjun, Marks: 85
102: Priya, Marks: 95
103: Kiran, Marks: 90

codeExamples.title: File Size Calculation and End-of-File Detection
codeExamples.explanation: To find the file size, seek to the end and use tellg() to get the position. After reading all content, any further read sets the EOF flag. eof() checks if end-of-file was reached, fail() checks for logical errors, and good() returns true only if no flags are set.
codeExamples.output:
File size: 11 bytes
Content: Hello World
Reached end of file
Read operation failed (expected at EOF)
Stream is not in good state

commonMistakes.title: Not Checking if File Opened Successfully
commonMistakes.error: If the file does not exist, the ifstream is in a failed state. The while loop never executes, but there is no error message. The program silently does nothing.
commonMistakes.explanation: Always check is_open() or the stream's boolean state after opening a file. File operations can fail for many reasons: file does not exist, permission denied, disk full. Silent failures are the hardest bugs to track.

commonMistakes.title: Using eof() in the Loop Condition
commonMistakes.error: The eof flag is set only AFTER a read operation fails, not when the last successful read reaches the end. This causes the last line to be processed twice because the loop executes one extra time.
commonMistakes.explanation: Use the read operation itself as the loop condition. getline and >> return a reference to the stream, which converts to false when a read fails. This is the correct and idiomatic pattern in C++.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting ios::binary for Struct I/O
commonMistakes.error: In text mode, the runtime may translate certain byte values (like 0x0A which is newline). This can corrupt binary data, especially on Windows.
commonMistakes.explanation: Always use ios::binary when reading or writing raw bytes with write() and read(). Binary mode prevents any character translation, ensuring the exact bytes you write are the exact bytes you read back.

commonMistakes.title: Mixing >> and getline Without Clearing the Buffer
commonMistakes.error: The >> operator leaves the newline character in the buffer. The subsequent getline reads this leftover newline as an empty line instead of the actual name.
commonMistakes.explanation: After using >>, always call file.ignore() to consume the remaining newline before using getline. This is the same issue that occurs with cin >> followed by getline(cin, ...).

summary: C++ file handling uses three classes from <fstream>: ifstream (read), ofstream (write), and fstream (read+write). They inherit the same interface as cin and cout.
Files can be opened via the constructor or the open() method. Always check is_open() to verify the file was successfully opened.
File modes control behavior: ios::in (read), ios::out (write/truncate), ios::app (append), ios::binary (no text translation), ios::trunc (truncate). Combine modes with the | operator.
Read lines with getline(file, string) and words with file >> variable. Use getline for lines containing spaces.
The correct loop pattern is while(getline(file, line)) or while(file >> var). Never use while(!file.eof()) as the loop condition -- it processes the last item twice.
Binary I/O uses write() and read() with reinterpret_cast<char*>. Always open in ios::binary mode for binary operations to prevent character translation.
seekg/seekp move the read/write position. tellg/tellp report the current position. Use seekg(0, ios::end) followed by tellg() to get file size.
For binary files with fixed-size records, random access to record N uses seekg(N * sizeof(Record)). This enables O(1) lookup, update, and delete operations.
CSV parsing combines getline (read full lines) with stringstream and getline(ss, token, ',') to split by delimiter.
After using >> to read formatted input, call file.ignore() before getline to consume the leftover newline character in the buffer.

topicSpecific.hint: endl writes a newline and flushes the buffer.
topicSpecific.explanation: The << operator writes text to the file just like cout. endl inserts a newline. The file will contain "Hello\nWorld\n".

topicSpecific.hint: Default ofstream mode is ios::out which truncates the file.
topicSpecific.explanation: Opening with default ofstream mode truncates the file. The second open erases "Line 1" and writes "Line 2". Only "Line 2" remains.

topicSpecific.hint: ios::app means append -- do not truncate.
topicSpecific.explanation: The first write creates "Line 1". The second open uses ios::app, so "Line 2" is appended after "Line 1" instead of overwriting.
The >> operator reads integers separated by whitespace. a=10, b=20, c=30. Sum = 60.
seekg(5) moves the read position to byte 5 (0-indexed), which is 'F'. After reading one character, tellg() returns 6.
Opening a nonexistent file fails. is_open() returns false (0). good() returns false (0) because the stream is in a failed state. fail() returns true (1).
The >> operator reads whitespace-separated tokens: "Arjun", "Kumar", "85". Three tokens are read.

topicSpecific.hint: getline reads the entire line including spaces.
topicSpecific.explanation: getline reads the entire line "Arjun Kumar 85" as a single string. There is one line, so count = 1.

topicSpecific.hint: Binary write stores the exact bytes of the struct.
topicSpecific.explanation: Binary write stores the raw bytes of the struct. Binary read loads those exact bytes back into a struct of the same type. The values are preserved exactly.

topicSpecific.hint: seekg(0, ios::end) moves to the end of the file. tellg() returns the position.
topicSpecific.explanation: "Hello World" is 11 characters. Seeking to the end and calling tellg() returns the file size in bytes: 11.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between ios::app and ios::ate?
topicSpecific.hint: Both position at the end, but they differ in what happens during writes.
topicSpecific.answer: ios::app (append) always writes to the end of the file, regardless of any seekp calls. Every write operation is guaranteed to append. ios::ate (at end) positions the cursor at the end when the file is opened, but you can seekp to any position and write there. ios::app is safer for log files where you always want to append.
topicSpecific.explanation: With ios::app, even if you call seekp(0), the next write still goes to the end. With ios::ate, seekp is honored. Use ios::app for logs and append-only files.

topicSpecific.question: Why should you never use while(!file.eof()) as a loop condition?
topicSpecific.hint: Think about when the eof flag is actually set.
topicSpecific.answer: The eof() flag is set only after a read operation fails, not when the last successful read reaches the end of data. Using while(!eof()) causes the loop to execute one extra time after the last valid read: the read fails, the old data is processed again, and then eof is detected. The correct pattern is while(getline(file, line)) or while(file >> var), where the read result is the condition.
topicSpecific.explanation: This is one of the most common C++ file handling bugs. The stream operator returns the stream reference, which converts to false when the read fails, making it the ideal loop condition.

mixed.hint: There is no endl between Hello and World.
mixed.explanation: "Hello " (6 chars) + "World" (5 chars) = "Hello World" (11 chars). Since there is no endl, everything is on one line.

mixed.answer: Nothing is written anywhere. The stream is in a failed state because no file was opened. The << operation silently fails.
mixed.explanation: Without calling open() or using the constructor with a filename, the ofstream has no associated file. All write operations fail silently. Always open a file before writing.
The file contains "1 2 3 4 5 ". The loop reads five integers (1+2+3+4+5 = 15). The trailing space does not cause issues because >> skips whitespace.

mixed.hint: find() returns string::npos if the substring is not found.
mixed.explanation: The loop reads 2 lines. The first line ("Name: Arjun") does not contain "Age". The second line ("Age: 20") does, so it is printed. Total lines = 2.
A Point struct has two ints, each typically 4 bytes, so sizeof(Point) = 8. The binary file contains exactly 8 bytes.

mixed.hint: This uses the problematic while(!eof()) pattern.
mixed.explanation: The file contains "0 1 2 ". The loop reads 0, 1, 2 successfully. After reading 2, eof is not yet set (the newline has been consumed but eof is not triggered until the next read attempt). The next in >> val fails and sets eof, but val still holds 2 from the previous read, so 2 is printed again. This is exactly why while(!eof()) is wrong.

mixed.question: What is the difference between ifstream, ofstream, and fstream?
mixed.hint: Think about read-only, write-only, and read-write.
mixed.answer: ifstream opens files for reading only (input). ofstream opens files for writing only (output, truncates by default). fstream can do both reading and writing, but you must specify the mode explicitly (e.g., ios::in | ios::out). Use the most restrictive type that meets your needs.
mixed.explanation: Using ifstream when you only need to read prevents accidental writes. Using ofstream when you only need to write is simpler than fstream. Use fstream only when you need both read and write access in the same stream.

mixed.question: What is reinterpret_cast<char*> used for in binary file I/O?
mixed.hint: write() and read() expect a char* pointer.
mixed.answer: write() and read() take a char* (pointer to bytes) and a byte count. reinterpret_cast<char*> converts a pointer to any type (struct, int, etc.) into a char*, telling the compiler to treat the memory as raw bytes. This allows writing the raw memory representation of any object to a file.
mixed.explanation: Binary I/O operates at the byte level. reinterpret_cast is necessary because write and read do not know about your struct's fields -- they just copy sizeof(T) bytes to/from the file.

mixed.question: Why is random access possible in binary files with fixed-size records but not in text files with variable-length lines?
mixed.hint: Think about how you calculate the byte position of record N.
mixed.answer: In binary files with fixed-size records (e.g., all records are sizeof(Student) = 64 bytes), the byte offset of record N is exactly N * sizeof(Student). You can seekg to that position in O(1). In text files, each line can have a different length ("Arjun" is 5 chars, "Raghunath" is 9 chars), so you cannot compute the byte offset of line N without reading all preceding lines. Text files require sequential access.
mixed.explanation: This is why databases and binary formats use fixed-size records or index structures. Random access requires knowing the exact byte position, which is only trivial with fixed-size records.

mcqs.question: Which header provides ifstream, ofstream, and fstream?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The <fstream> header provides all file stream classes: ifstream, ofstream, and fstream.

mcqs.question: What is the default mode for ofstream?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. ofstream defaults to ios::out, which truncates the file if it exists. Use ios::app to append instead.

mcqs.question: Which function reads an entire line including spaces from a file?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. getline(file, line) reads until the newline, including spaces. The >> operator stops at whitespace.

mcqs.question: What does file.is_open() return if the file was not found?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. is_open() returns false if the file could not be opened for any reason (not found, permission denied, etc.).

mcqs.question: What does ios::app mode do?

options.A: Opens the file in binary mode
options.C: Appends all writes to the end of the file

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. ios::app ensures every write goes to the end of the file, preserving existing content.

mcqs.question: What does seekg(0, ios::end) followed by tellg() give you?

options.A: The first character of the file
options.B: The last character of the file
options.D: The number of lines in the file

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Seeking to the end and calling tellg() returns the byte position at the end, which equals the file size.

mcqs.question: What does reinterpret_cast<char*>(&obj) do?

options.A: Converts the object to a string
options.B: Converts the object's memory address to a char pointer for byte-level access
options.C: Creates a copy of the object

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. reinterpret_cast<char*> tells the compiler to treat the memory at that address as raw bytes (char*). This is required for write() and read() which operate on byte buffers.

mcqs.question: What is the correct way to read integers from a file until end?

options.D: while (true) { file >> num; if (file.eof()) break; process(num); }

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. file >> num returns the stream, which converts to false when the read fails. This is the idiomatic and correct pattern. Options A and C process the last value twice.

mcqs.question: What is the difference between seekg and seekp?

options.A: seekg is for text files, seekp is for binary files
options.B: seekg sets the read position, seekp sets the write position
options.C: seekg is absolute, seekp is relative

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. seekg (seek get) positions the read pointer. seekp (seek put) positions the write pointer. In an fstream, both may be needed.

mcqs.question: Why must you use ios::binary when using write() and read() for struct I/O?

options.B: Text mode may translate certain bytes (like newline), corrupting the data
options.C: write() and read() only work in binary mode

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. In text mode, the runtime may translate to (Windows) or vice versa. If the struct's raw bytes happen to contain 0x0A ( byte), this translation corrupts the data. Binary mode performs no translation.

mcqs.question: How do you calculate the byte position of the Nth record in a binary file with fixed-size records?

options.C: You cannot calculate it; you must read sequentially

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. With 0-based indexing, record N starts at byte position N * sizeof(Record). Use seekg with this value for O(1) random access.

mcqs.question: What happens if you open a file with ofstream (default mode) that already contains data?

options.A: New data is appended after existing data
options.B: The file is truncated (existing data is erased) before writing
options.D: New data overwrites starting at position 0 but does not truncate

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The default mode for ofstream is ios::out, which implies ios::trunc. The file is truncated to zero length before any new data is written.

mcqs.question: How do you combine file modes in C++?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. File modes are combined using the bitwise OR operator (|). Example: ios::in | ios::out | ios::binary.

mcqs.question: What does file.close() do?

options.B: Flushes the buffer and releases the file handle
options.C: Resets the read position to the beginning

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. close() flushes any buffered data to disk and releases the OS file handle. The file remains on disk.

codingChallenges.title: Word Frequency Counter from File
codingChallenges.description: Read a text file word by word and count the frequency of each word. Print the words and their counts in alphabetical order. Use a map to store counts.
codingChallenges.sampleInput: File content: "the cat sat on the mat the cat"
codingChallenges.constraints: Use ifstream with >> to read words. Use map<string, int> for counting.

codingChallenges.title: Student Marks CSV Parser and Topper Finder
codingChallenges.description: Write a CSV file with student name, maths, physics, chemistry marks. Read the CSV, calculate total marks for each student, and find the topper. Use stringstream to parse comma-separated values.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput:
Arjun: Total=253
Priya: Total=275
Kiran: Total=240
Topper: Priya (275)
codingChallenges.constraints: Use getline for reading lines, stringstream for parsing. Do not hardcode the number of students.

codingChallenges.title: Binary Student Database with Search and Update
codingChallenges.description: Implement a student database using binary file I/O. Support: (1) Add student record, (2) Display all records, (3) Search by roll number, (4) Update marks by roll number. Use seekg/seekp for in-place updates.
codingChallenges.sampleInput: Add {101, Arjun, 85}, {102, Priya, 78}. Update roll 102 marks to 95. Display all.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use fixed-size struct with char array for name (not string). Use seekg/seekp for O(1) updates.

codingChallenges.title: Config File Parser with Default Values
codingChallenges.description: Write a config file parser that reads key=value pairs, skips comment lines (starting with #) and empty lines. Provide a get method that returns a default value if the key is not found.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput:
host = localhost
port = 3306
debug = true
timeout = 30 (default)
codingChallenges.constraints: Use map<string, string> for storage. Support # comments and empty lines. Provide a get(key, default) method.

codingChallenges.title: File-based Leaderboard System
codingChallenges.description: Create a leaderboard system that stores player names and scores in a text file. Support adding new scores, reading all scores, sorting by score (descending), and displaying the top N players. The file persists between program runs.
codingChallenges.sampleInput: Add: Arjun 850, Priya 920, Kiran 780, Neha 920. Display top 3.
codingChallenges.constraints: Read from file, sort in memory, display. Handle the case where the file does not exist.
